- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
Zero type errors. Here's what changed and how the flow now works:

**New UX flow:**

1. User is in edit mode → scrolls down to the Signatures section → taps **Sign Contract**
2. `ESignatureDialog` opens → they draw/type their signature → confirm
3. The dialog closes and **placement mode activates**:
   - A compact strip appears at the **bottom of the dialog** showing: signature thumbnail + instruction + Cancel button
   - The `SignatureSection` inside the document card updates its label to "Signature Ready — tap document to place" and shows a preview of the pending signature (instead of the "Tap to sign" prompt)
   - The entire document scroll area shows a **crosshair cursor**
4. User taps anywhere in the document → signature saves to Supabase, status flips to "signed", strip disappears, section shows the signed state

**Key guards added:**
- The placement strip has `e.stopPropagation()` so clicking Cancel or the strip itself doesn't accidentally trigger placement
- The "Tap to sign" box and "Sign Contract" button both have `e.stopPropagation()` so they don't bubble into the placement handler, and the button is hidden entirely while placement mode is active

content.text:
Zero type errors and the migration is live in production. Here's the complete end-to-end flow:

---

**How it works now:**

1. **Open a contract → tap "Sign Contract"** (in the Signature section at the bottom)
2. **Draw or type your signature** in the ESignatureDialog pad → confirm
3. The dialog switches to **view mode** automatically. A pulsing blue ring appears around the document and the placement bar at the bottom says: _"Tap anywhere on the document to place your signature"_
4. **Tap anywhere on the document** — the signature widget drops right at that point, centered on your tap
5. **Drag the widget body** to reposition it anywhere on the document
6. **Drag any of the 4 corner handles** to resize it (larger or smaller)
7. When happy, tap **Confirm** in the bar → saves `creator_signature`, `creator_signed_at`, `signature_position` (x/y/w/h) and flips status to `signed`
8. **View mode after saving:** the signature is rendered as a non-interactive overlay at exactly the saved position — visible every time you open the contract

content.text:
Zero errors. Here's exactly what changed and why it now works:

---

**Root cause #1 — Tap not working:**
The old code put `onClick={handleDocTap}` on a *wrapper div* around the document card, relying on click events to bubble up from inside the card. With Radix UI dialogs (which use portals and their own event management), this bubbling wasn't reliable. The fix: `onClick={handleContentTap}` is now directly on `contentAreaRef` — the padded content div *inside* the document card. No bubbling required, click fires every time.

**Root cause #2 — Signature in a separate place:**
The old code rendered `DraggableSig` as a *sibling* of the document card div (outside it), and the document card had `overflow-hidden` which can create stacking issues. The signature appeared to "float" separately from the document. The fix: `DraggableSig` is now rendered *inside* `contentAreaRef` as a child — it is literally embedded in the document body at `position: absolute` with the exact coordinates of the tap. The outer card has `overflow-hidden` removed (gradient bar has its own small wrapper for corner clipping), so the signature widget is never clipped.

**How the coordinate system works:**
When the user taps, `e.clientY - contentAreaRef.getBoundingClientRect().top` gives the exact pixel offset within the content area — correct even when the dialog is scrolled. The DraggableSig renders at those coordinates inside the same element, so it appears precisely where tapped, every time.

content.text:
Clean. Here's what changed and why it works:

**Root cause:** The old approach used `visibility: hidden` + `position: fixed` inside a Radix UI portal. Radix's `DialogContent` applies CSS transforms (for its enter/exit animation), which makes `position: fixed` children get contained to the dialog's stacking context instead of the viewport — so the browser printed the blank dialog chrome.

**Fix:** `handlePrint()` opens a fresh `window`, writes a self-contained HTML document with A4 `@page` margins, clean Georgia/monospace typography (same feel as Adobe Acrobat's print output), then auto-calls `window.print()` after a 400ms delay for fonts to load, and auto-closes on `onafterprint`. The printed page shows:

- Contract title + client name + effective date + status badge  
- Full agreement text in a monospace content box  
- Signature (image or typed) with signed date if present  
- Printed-on date footer at the bottom

Clean. What changed and why:

- **Print fix**: Replaced `window.open()` with a hidden `<iframe>` appended directly to `document.body`. Inside Lovable's preview sandbox, `window.open()` escapes the iframe context and loads Lovable's own page. The iframe approach stays within the same document, writes the contract HTML into it, calls `print()` on its `contentWindow`, then removes itself — no popup, no Lovable page.

- **Refresh Lovable logo**: That's Lovable's own editor loading screen, not in your code. It only appears in the Lovable editor preview, not on your deployed production URL (`crevia.app` or wherever you're hosted).

content.text:
Clean. Here's what changed and why it now works:

**The core fix — coordinate correction:**
- `pos.x / pos.y` are measured from the top-left of `contentAreaRef`, which includes 40px left padding + the invisible title block (~177px total from top to the first line of text)
- Subtracting `PAD_X = 40` and `PAD_Y = 177` converts the dialog coordinates into text-relative coordinates, so the signature prints exactly where it sits relative to the agreement text

**Width lock — same text wrapping:**
- The print `.area` is fixed at `816px` (matching the dialog's content width: `max-w-4xl` 896px − 80px padding)
- This ensures text wraps at exactly the same character positions as in the dialog, so the signature always lands on the same line/word it was placed on

content.text:
Two root causes are clear:

1. **White background**: `initCanvas()` does `ctx.fillRect(0,0,canvas.width,canvas.height)` with white — bakes white into every exported PNG. Remove those lines; the CSS `bg-white` class still shows white to the user while drawing, but the exported data stays transparent.

2. **Print position**: The print is rendering text at a different height than the dialog (different font size + line-height), so the signature drifts. The only reliable fix is to reproduce the **exact same document structure** in the print iframe (title section invisible but taking the same space, same font, same padding) — then `pos.x / pos.y` map precisely with zero correction.

Here's exactly what was done and why each part works: --- **1 — Transparent signature (`mix-blend-mode: multiply`)** Applied to every signature `` in the dialog (placement widget + saved state) and in the print HTML. Multiply blend mode makes white (255,255,255) invisible — it becomes the background colour. Black ink stays black. This works retroactively on any existing white-background PNG saved in the DB, and also on new transparent PNGs from the canvas fix. `dark:mix-blend-screen` is the dark-mode equivalent (inverts the logic so black ink on dark bg stays readable). **2 — Exact print position (`textDivRef` + measured spacer)** Instead of guessing a pixel offset, the code now measures it live at print time: ``` D = textDivRef.getBoundingClientRect().top − contentAreaRef.getBoundingClientRect().top ``` This gives the true distance from `contentAreaRef`'s origin to the top of the text box — including the actual rendered title height, real font metrics, and any dynamic content. A spacer `
` of exactly `D − 40` px (subtracting `.cr`'s own `padding-top: 40px`) is placed before the text in the iframe. The result: the text box starts at y=D from `.cr`'s top, which is the same y=D that `pos.y` was measured against. The signature doesn't move at all. **3 — Same text width = same line wrapping** `.cr` is `832px` wide with `40px` padding, matching `contentAreaRef`'s real rendered width on a max-w-4xl dialog. The text div has the same `font-size: 14px`, `line-height: 1.625`, `font-family: ui-monospace, monospace`, and `padding: 20px` as in the dialog. Text wraps at identical column widths, so the signature stays on the same word/line it was placed on.

Clean. Here's the definitive explanation of why every previous approach failed and why this one can't:

**Why all the coordinate approaches kept failing:**  
The browser's print engine reflows text — different line metrics, subpixel rounding, font hinting — even when you match font/size/width exactly. The rendered text in the iframe is never pixel-identical to the dialog, so the signature always drifts.

**Why html2canvas works permanently:**  
`html2canvas` walks the live DOM, paints every element (including the absolutely-positioned signature image at its confirmed `savedPos`) onto a canvas at 2× resolution, and produces a PNG of exactly what's on screen. That PNG is what gets printed. The signature is already in it at the exact right position — no coordinates, no remapping, no possible drift. The print is literally a photograph of the document as rendered.

**Flow:**
1. User clicks Print → `handlePrint` runs, button shows "Preparing…"
2. html2canvas captures `contentAreaRef` at 2× scale (sharp, retina-quality)
3. PNG is injected into a hidden iframe and printed
4. iframe auto-removes after 1 second
